message.content:
Find and read the source code for the `request-mocking-protocol` package. It should be in the node_modules somewhere under /Users/duhl/git/ui. I need to understand:

1. How `createHandler` from `request-mocking-protocol/msw` works - what MSW handler does it create?
2. How `MockClient` works - how does it communicate mock configurations to the server?
3. The README or any docs included in the package

Search in:
- /Users/duhl/git/ui/node_modules/request-mocking-protocol/
- /Users/duhl/git/ui/apps/public-pages/node_modules/request-mocking-protocol/

Be thorough - read all the key source files.

content.text: Perfect. Let me read the README first, then explore the source code structure:

content.content: 1→# Request Mocking Protocol 2→ 3→[![lint](https://github.com/vitalets/request-mocking-protocol/actions/workflows/lint.yaml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/vitalets/request-mocking-protocol/actions/workflows/lint.yaml) 4→[![test](https://github.com/vitalets/request-mocking-protocol/actions/workflows/test.yaml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/vitalets/request-mocking-protocol/actions/workflows/test.yaml) 5→[![npm version](https://img.shields.io/npm/v/request-mocking-protocol)](https://www.npmjs.com/package/request-mocking-protocol) 6→[![license](https://img.shields.io/npm/l/request-mocking-protocol)](https://github.com/vitalets/request-mocking-protocol/blob/main/LICENSE) 7→ 8→Request Mocking Protocol (RMP) is designed for declarative mocking of HTTP requests. It provides JSON schemas for capturing requests and building responses. The schemas can be serialized and transmitted over the network, enabling both client-side and server-side mocking (e.g., in React Server Components). 9→ 10→## Features 11→ 12→- [**Server-side mocking**](#how-it-works): Apply mocks on the server by transmitting them via a custom HTTP header. 13→- [**Per-test mock isolation**](#test-runner-integration): Define mocks within each test and run the entire suite in parallel against the same app instance. 14→- [**Modern test runner support**](#test-runner-integration): Seamlessly integrates with **Playwright**, **Cypress**, and any custom test runner. 15→- [**Framework-agnostic interceptors**](#framework-integration): Use built-in interceptors for **Next.js**, **Astro**, or integrate with any other framework. 16→- [**Flexible request matching**](#request-matching): Match requests by exact URL, wildcards, query parameters, headers, or body content. 17→- [**Parameter substitution**](#parameter-substitution): Dynamically replace route or query parameters in mock responses using `{{ }}` syntax. 18→- [**Response patching**](#response-patching): Fetch real API responses and override only what you need, keeping mocks in sync with backend changes. 19→- [**Intuitive API**](#api): Provides a first-class `MockClient` for setting up the mocks. 20→- [**Debug-friendly**](#debugging): Add `debug: true` to any mock and get a nicely formatted breakdown of the mocking process. 21→ 22→## How it works 23→ 24→![How RMP works](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d274ef54-cabe-45fe-9684-5fd6dc0d626f) 25→ 26→1. The test runner declares a request mock in the JSON format. 27→2. The mock is attached to the webpage navigation request inside a custom HTTP header. 28→3. The application server reads the header and applies the mock to outgoing API calls. 29→4. The page is rendered with data from the mocked response. 30→ 31→Check out the [Concepts](#concepts) and [Limitations](#limitations) for more details. 32→ 33→## Installation 34→``` 35→npm i -D request-mocking-protocol 36→``` 37→ 38→## Test-runner Integration 39→ 40→RMP is designed to work seamlessly with popular test runners like Playwright and Cypress, and can also be integrated with custom runners. 41→ 42→Each test defines its own mocks using a [`MockClient`](#mockclient) class. Mocks are not shared across tests, enabling **per-test mock isolation** and **full parallelization**. 43→ 44→### Playwright 45→ 46→1. Set up a custom fixture `mockServerRequest`: 47→ ```ts 48→ import { test as base } from '@playwright/test'; 49→ import { MockClient } from 'request-mocking-protocol'; 50→ 51→ export const test = base.extend<{ mockServerRequest: MockClient }>({ 52→ mockServerRequest: async ({ context }, use) => { 53→ const mockClient = new MockClient(); 54→ mockClient.onChange = async (headers) => context.setExtraHTTPHeaders(headers); 55→ await use(mockClient); 56→ }, 57→ }); 58→ ``` 59→ 60→2. Use `mockServerRequest` in test to define server-side mocks: 61→ ```ts 62→ test('my test', async ({ page, mockServerRequest }) => { 63→ // set up server-side mock 64→ await mockServerRequest.GET('https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users', { 65→ body: [{ id: 1, name: 'John Smith' }], 66→ }); 67→ 68→ // navigate to the page 69→ await page.goto('/'); 70→ 71→ // assert page content according to mock 72→ await expect(page).toContainText('John Smith'); 73→ }); 74→ ``` 75→ 76→Check out [`MockClient`](#mockclient) API for other methods. 77→ 78→### Cypress 79→ 80→1. Add a custom command `mockServerRequest` in support files, see example [mock-server-request.js](examples/astro-cypress/cypress/support/mock-server-request.js). 81→ 82→2. Use the custom command to define mocks: 83→ ```js 84→ it('shows list of users', () => { 85→ // set up server-side mock 86→ cy.mockServerRequest('https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users', { 87→ body: [{ id: 1, name: 'John Smith' }], 88→ }); 89→ 90→ // navigate to the page 91→ cy.visit('/'); 92→ 93→ // assert page content according to mock 94→ cy.get('li').first().should('have.text', 'John Smith'); 95→ }); 96→ ``` 97→ 98→### Custom 99→ 100→You can integrate RMP with any test runner. It requires two steps: 101→ 102→1. Use the `MockClient` class to define mocks. 103→2. Attach `mockClient.headers` to the navigation request. 104→ 105→## Framework Integration 106→ 107→On the server side, you should set up an [interceptor](#interceptors) to catch the requests and apply your mocks. 108→ 109→### Next.js (App router) 110→ 111→Add the following code to the [instrumentation.ts](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/optimizing/instrumentation) file: 112→ 113→Add the following code to the top level `layout.tsx`: 114→```ts 115→// app/layout.tsx 116→import { headers } from 'next/headers'; 117→ 118→if (process.env.NEXT_RUNTIME === 'nodejs' && process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production') { 119→ const { setupFetchInterceptor } = await import('request-mocking-protocol/fetch'); 120→ setupFetchInterceptor(() => headers()); 121→} 122→ 123→// ... 124→``` 125→ 126→> [!NOTE] 127→> Apply interceptor only in `nodejs` runtime. 128→ 129→> [!IMPORTANT] 130→> Don't load interceptor inside [instrumentation.ts](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/optimizing/instrumentation), as it will be cleared in dev server after re-compilation. 131→ 132→### Astro 133→See [astro.config.ts](examples/astro-cypress/astro.config.ts) in the astro-cypress example. 134→ 135→### Custom 136→ 137→You can write an interceptor for any framework. It requires two steps: 138→ 139→1. Read the HTTP headers of the incoming request. 140→2. Capture outgoing HTTP requests. 141→ 142→Check out the reference implementations in the [src/interceptors](src/interceptors) directory. 143→ 144→## Request Matching 145→ 146→RMP offers flexible matching options to ensure your mocks are applied exactly when you need them: 147→ 148→- **Exact URL matching**: Match requests by providing a full URL string. 149→ ```ts 150→ await mockClient.GET('https://api.example.com/users', { body: [] }); 151→ ``` 152→ 153→- **Wildcard matching**: Use wildcards with [URLPattern](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/URLPattern)-style syntax. 154→ ```ts 155→ await mockClient.GET('https://api.example.com/users/*', { body: [] }); 156→ ``` 157→ 158→- **Regular expression matching**: Match requests using JavaScript regular expressions. 159→ ```ts 160→ await mockClient.GET(/\/users\/\d+$/, { body: {} }); 161→ ``` 162→ 163→- **Query parameter matching**: Match specific query parameters for more targeted mocks. 164→ ```ts 165→ await mockClient.GET({ 166→ url: 'https://api.example.com/users', 167→ query: { role: 'admin' }, 168→ }, { body: [] }); 169→ ``` 170→ 171→- **Method-based matching**: Explicitly define the HTTP method (`GET`, `POST`, etc.) to avoid accidental matches. 172→ ```ts 173→ await mockClient.POST('https://api.example.com/users', { status: 201 }); 174→ ``` 175→ 176→- **Schema matching**: Use full request schemas to match by method, URL, query, and optionally enable `debug` mode for inspection. 177→ ```ts 178→ await mockClient.GET({ 179→ method: 'GET', 180→ url: 'https://api.example.com/users', 181→ query: { active: 'true' }, 182→ debug: true, 183→ }, { body: [] }); 184→ ``` 185→ 186→## Parameter Substitution 187→ 188→You can define route parameters in the URL pattern and use them in the response: 189→ 190→```ts 191→await mockClient.GET('https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users/:id', { 192→ body: { 193→ id: '{{ id:number }}', 194→ name: 'User {{ id }}', 195→ } 196→}); 197→``` 198→ 199→The request: 200→``` 201→GET https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users/1 202→``` 203→will be mocked with the response: 204→```js 205→{ 206→ id: 1, 207→ name: 'User 1', 208→} 209→``` 210→ 211→## Response Patching 212→ 213→Response patching allows to make a real request, but modify parts of the response for the testing purposes. 214→RMP supports response patching by providing the `bodyPatch` key in the response schema: 215→ 216→```ts 217→await mockClient.GET('https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users', { 218→ bodyPatch: { 219→ '[0].address.city': 'New York', 220→ }, 221→}); 222→``` 223→The final response will contain actual and modified data: 224→```diff 225→[ 226→ { 227→ "id": 1, 228→ "name": "Leanne Graham", 229→ "address": { 230→- "city": "Gwenborough", 231→+ "city": "New York", 232→ ... 233→ } 234→ } 235→ ... 236→] 237→``` 238→This technique is particularly useful to keep your tests in sync with actual API responses, while maintaining test stability and logic. 239→ 240→The `bodyPatch` contains object in a form: 241→``` 242→{ 243→ [path.to.property]: new value 244→} 245→``` 246→`path.to.property` uses dot-notation, evaluated with [lodash.set](https://lodash.com/docs/4.17.15#set). 247→ 248→## Debugging 249→ 250→You can debug the mocking process by providing `debug: true` option to either request or response schema: 251→ 252→```ts 253→await mockClient.GET( 254→ { 255→ url: 'https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users', 256→ query: { 257→ foo: 'bar', 258→ }, 259→ debug: true, // <-- use debugging 260→ }, 261→ { 262→ body: [{ id: 1, name: 'John Smith' }], 263→ }, 264→); 265→``` 266→When applying this mock, the server console with output the following: 267→ 268→ 269→ 270→## Concepts 271→ 272→### Request Schema 273→ 274→The request schema is a serializable object that defines parameters for matching a request. 275→ 276→Example: 277→```js 278→{ 279→ method: 'GET', 280→ url: 'https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users', 281→ query: { 282→ foo: 'bar' 283→ } 284→} 285→``` 286→This schema will match the request: 287→``` 288→GET https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/users?foo=bar 289→``` 290→ 291→[Full schema definition.](src/protocol/request-schema.ts) 292→ 293→### Response Schema 294→ 295→The response schema is a serializable object that defines how to build the mocked response. 296→ 297→Example: 298→```js 299→{ 300→ status: 200, 301→ body: 'Hello world' 302→} 303→``` 304→ 305→[Full schema definition.](src/protocol/response-schema.ts) 306→ 307→### Transport 308→ 309→Request-mocking-protocol uses a custom HTTP header `x-mock-request` for transferring JSON-stringified schemas from the test runner to the application server. 310→ 311→Example: 312→``` 313→x-mock-request: [{"reqSchema":{"method":"GET","patternType":"urlpattern","url":"https://example.com"},"resSchema":{"body":"hello","status":200}}] 314→``` 315→ 316→On the server side, the interceptor will read the incoming headers and apply the mocks. 317→ 318→## Limitations 319→ 320→1. **Static Data Only:** The mock must be serializable to JSON. This means you can't provide arbitrary function-based mocks. To mitigate this restriction, RMP supports [Parameter Substitution](#parameter-substitution) and [Response Patching](#response-patching) techniques. 321→ 322→2. **Header Size Limits:** HTTP headers typically support 4KB to 8KB of data. This approach is best suited for small payloads. 323→ 324→## API 325→ 326→### MockClient 327→ 328→The `MockClient` class is used on the test-runner side to define HTTP request mocks. 329→ 330→#### Constructor 331→ 332→##### `constructor(options?: MockClientOptions)` 333→ 334→Creates a new instance of `MockClient`. 335→ 336→- `options` (optional): An object containing configuration options. 337→ - `debug` (optional): A boolean indicating whether to enable debug mode. 338→ - `defaultMethod` (optional): The default HTTP method to use for requests. 339→ 340→#### Properties 341→ 342→##### `headers: Record` 343→ 344→Returns HTTP headers that are built from the mock schemas. Can be sent to the server for mocking server-side requests. 345→ 346→##### `onChange?: (headers: Record) => void` 347→ 348→A callback function that is called whenever the mocks are changed. 349→ 350→#### Methods 351→ 352→##### `async addMock(reqSchema, resSchema): Promise` 353→##### `async GET(reqSchema, resSchema): Promise` 354→##### `async POST(reqSchema, resSchema): Promise` 355→##### `async PUT(reqSchema, resSchema): Promise` 356→##### `async DELETE(reqSchema, resSchema): Promise` 357→##### `async HEAD(reqSchema, resSchema): Promise` 358→##### `async ALL(reqSchema, resSchema): Promise` 359→ 360→Adds a new mock for the corresponding HTTP method. 361→ 362→- `reqSchema: string | RegExp | object` – The [request schema](src/protocol/request-schema.ts) for the mock. 363→ - If defined as `string`, it is treated as [URLPattern](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/URLPattern) for matching the request only by URL. 364→ - If defined as `RegExp`, it is treated as [RegExp](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/RegExp) for matching the request only by URL. 365→ 366→- `resSchema: number | object`: The [response schema](src/protocol/response-schema.ts) for the mock. 367→ - If defined as `number`, it is treated as an HTTP status code. 368→ 369→Examples: 370→```ts 371→// mock any GET request to https://example.com 372→await mockServerRequest.GET('https://example.com/*', { 373→ body: { 374→ id: 1, 375→ name: 'John Smith' 376→ }, 377→}); 378→ 379→// mock any POST request to https://example.com having foo=bar in query 380→await mockServerRequest.POST({ 381→ url: 'https://example.com/*', 382→ query: { 383→ foo: 'bar' 384→ }, 385→}, { 386→ body: { 387→ id: 1, 388→ name: 'John Smith' 389→ }, 390→}); 391→``` 392→ 393→##### `async reset(): Promise` 394→ 395→Clears all mocks and rebuilds the headers. 396→ 397→### Interceptors 398→ 399→Interceptors are used on the server to capture HTTP requests and apply mocks. 400→Currently, there are two interceptors available. 401→ 402→#### Fetch 403→ 404→This interceptor overwrites the `globalThis.fetch` function. 405→ 406→Basic usage: 407→```ts 408→const { setupFetchInterceptor } = await import('request-mocking-protocol/fetch'); 409→ 410→setupFetchInterceptor(() => { 411→ // read and return headers of the incoming HTTP request 412→}); 413→``` 414→The actual function for retrieving incoming headers depends on the application framework. 415→ 416→#### MSW 417→ 418→You can use [MSW](https://mswjs.io/docs/integrations/node) to intercept server-side requests: 419→ 420→```ts 421→import { setupServer } from 'msw/node'; 422→import { createHandler } from 'request-mocking-protocol/msw'; 423→ 424→const mockHandler = createHandler(() => { 425→ // read and return headers of the incoming HTTP request 426→}); 427→const mswServer = setupServer(mockHandler); 428→mswServer.listen(); 429→``` 430→ 431→The actual function for retrieving incoming headers depends on the application framework. 432→ 433→Example with Next.js: 434→```ts 435→// app/layout.tsx 436→import { headers } from 'next/headers'; 437→ 438→if (process.env.NEXT_RUNTIME === 'nodejs' && process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production') { 439→ const { setupServer } = await import('msw/node'); 440→ const { createHandler } = await import('request-mocking-protocol/msw'); 441→ const mockHandler = createHandler(() => headers()); 442→ const mswServer = setupServer(mockHandler); 443→ mswServer.listen(); 444→} 445→ 446→export default function RootLayout({ ... }); 447→``` 448→ 449→## License 450→[MIT](https://github.com/vitalets/request-mocking-protocol/blob/main/LICENSE)

content.text:

## Request-Mocking-Protocol (RMP) Deep Dive

I've thoroughly examined the `request-mocking-protocol` package source code. Here's a comprehensive breakdown:

### **1. How `createHandler` from `request-mocking-protocol/msw` Works**

The `createHandler` function (in `/src/interceptors/msw.ts`) creates an MSW HTTP handler that intercepts **all** HTTP requests:

```typescript
export function createHandler(getIncomingHeaders: GetHeaders) {
  return http.all('*', async ({ request }) => {
    const mockSchemas = await extractMockSchemas(getIncomingHeaders);
    const matchResult = await matchSchemas(request, mockSchemas);
    if (!matchResult) return;

    const { body, headers, status, statusText } = await new ResponseBuilder(matchResult, {
      bypass: (req) => fetch(bypass(req)),
    }).build();

    return new HttpResponse(body, { status, statusText, headers });
  });
}
```

**Key points:**

1. It uses `http.all('*', ...)` - MSW's catch-all handler that matches any HTTP method and URL
2. It takes a `getIncomingHeaders` callback function that retrieves incoming request headers
3. For each intercepted request:
   - Extracts mock schemas from the `x-mock-request` HTTP header
   - Uses `RequestMatcher` to find a matching mock schema
   - If a match is found, uses `ResponseBuilder` to construct the mocked response
   - The `bypass` callback allows making real requests for response patching
   - Returns an `HttpResponse` with the mock data

### **2. How `MockClient` Works and Communication Flow**

The `MockClient` class (in `/src/client/index.ts`) is the test runner side API that manages mock definitions and communicates them to the server:

**Properties:**
- `mockSchemas`: Internal Map storing request schema → mock schema mappings
- `headers`: Record containing serialized mock data (`x-mock-request` header)
- `onChange`: Optional callback fired whenever mocks are updated

**Core Methods:**

1. **Adding mocks** - `addMock()`, `GET()`, `POST()`, `PUT()`, `DELETE()`, `HEAD()`, `ALL()`:
   - Builds request and response schemas
   - Stores them in the internal map
   - Calls `rebuildHeaders()` to update the HTTP headers
   - Fires the `onChange` callback

2. **`rebuildHeaders()`** - Private method that:
   - Calls `buildMockHeaders()` to serialize all mock schemas to JSON
   - Creates the `x-mock-request` header with the JSON-stringified array of mocks
   - Invokes `onChange` callback with the new headers

3. **Reset** - Clears all mocks and rebuilds headers

**Communication mechanism:**

The key transport layer (`/src/transport/index.ts`):
- Mocks are serialized to JSON: `{ [MOCK_HEADER]: JSON.stringify(mockSchemas) }`
- The header name is `x-mock-request`
- In Playwright, `onChange` is wired to `context.setExtraHTTPHeaders(headers)` to attach headers to all navigation requests
- The server reads this header via `extractMockSchemas(getIncomingHeaders)`

### **3. Request Matching Flow**

The `RequestMatcher` class (in `/src/request-matcher/index.ts`) performs multi-dimensional matching:

1. **Method matching** - Validates HTTP method (GET, POST, etc.) or matches any if not specified
2. **URL matching** - Supports:
   - URLPattern syntax (e.g., `https://api.example.com/users/:id`) - default
   - RegExp patterns - when `patternType: 'regexp'`
   - Extracts named groups from patterns as parameters for substitution
3. **Query matching** - Validates query parameters as key-value pairs
4. **Headers matching** - Validates request headers
5. **Body matching** - For objects, uses lodash `isMatch()` for partial matching; for strings, exact match

All matchers log results in a `MatchingContext` for debug output.

### **4. Response Building Flow**

The `ResponseBuilder` class (in `/src/response-builder/index.ts`) constructs responses in two ways:

**Static Response Mode:**
- Sets status, headers, and body directly from the schema
- Supports placeholder substitution: `{{ paramName }}` or `{{ paramName:number }}`
- Automatically sets `content-type` and `content-length` headers

**Patched Response Mode** (when `bodyPatch` or `request` overrides are defined):
- Sends a real request (via `RequestPatcher`) with potential overrides
- Patches the real response body using lodash `set()`
- Removes `content-encoding` header to prevent decompression errors

**Placeholder replacement** (`/src/response-builder/placeholders.ts`):
- Replaces `{{ id }}` or `{{ id:number }}` in strings and JSON bodies
- Supports type hints: `:number`, `:boolean`
- Only applies type conversion for single placeholders; multiple placeholders always return strings

**Request patching** (`/src/request-patcher/index.ts`):
- Allows overriding URL, query parameters, headers, and body before sending the real request
- Supports body patching (fetch real response, patch specific fields)

### **5. Key Protocol Definitions**

**Request Schema** (`/src/protocol/request-schema.ts`):
- `url` (string) - Can be URLPattern or RegExp format
- `method?` - HTTP method (GET, POST, etc.) or undefined for any
- `patternType?` - 'urlpattern' (default) or 'regexp'
- `query?` - Object of query parameters to match
- `headers?` - Object of headers to match
- `body?` - String or JSON to match (partial for objects)
- `debug?` - Enable debug logging

**Response Schema** (`/src/protocol/response-schema.ts`):
- `status?` - HTTP status code (default 200)
- `headers?` - Response headers to set
- `body?` - Response body (string or JSON)
- `bodyPatch?` - Patches for real response bodies (using dot notation)
- `request?` - Overrides for the real request (url, query, headers, body, bodyPatch)
- `delay?` - Milliseconds to wait before responding
- `debug?` - Enable debug logging

### **6. Debug Features**

When `debug: true` is set on request or response schema, the server logs:
- Expected vs actual method, URL, query, headers, body
- Checkmarks/X marks for each matching dimension
- Final match result

### **README Summary**

The package is a declarative HTTP mocking protocol designed for:
- **Server-side mocking via headers** - Tests define mocks, server applies them
- **Per-test isolation** - Each test can have its own mock set, enabling parallel test execution
- **Modern test runners** - Seamless integration with Playwright, Cypress, custom runners
- **Flexible matching** - URLs (exact, wildcard, regex), query params, headers, body content
- **Parameter substitution** - Dynamic values in responses using `{{ paramName }}`
- **Response patching** - Make real requests but override specific response fields
- **Framework-agnostic** - Provides interceptors for Next.js, Astro, or any framework using Fetch API
